includedSources.name: Bond Beat (BofA, Barclays, BMO, DB, MS, UBS, CoT)

items.title: CFTC greenlights first US Bitcoin perp — starting gun fired on most contested market grab in crypto history
items.idea: CFTC approved Kalshi to offer the first true bitcoin-referenced perpetual (BTCPERP) for US users. Simultaneously, Coinbase received a no-action letter to connect US clients to global derivatives markets. Hyperliquid ($HYPE, $60B market cap) broke ATH on the news. Multiple well-capitalized players — Hyperliquid, Lighter, Coinbase, Kraken, Robinhood, OKX, Polymarket — are now racing for a US perp market that has been entirely offshore until now. Derivatives made up 88% of OKX total revenue in 2024.
items.angle: This is the single biggest US crypto regulatory unlock since spot ETFs. $HYPE is the obvious direct play, but Coinbase and Robinhood have distribution advantages. The competitive dynamics matter more than the first-mover advantage.
items.tweetA: CFTC just opened the US perps market. $HYPE breaks ATH. Coinbase gets a no-action letter to plug US clients into global derivatives. This is the Bitcoin ETF moment — except the addressable market is $85.7 trillion in annual volume.
items.tweetB: Kalshi is first. But watch Hyperliquid, Coinbase, Kraken, OKX, Robinhood all filing next. Derivative volume was 88% of OKX's revenue in 2024. The US market was zero of that. Do the math on what's coming.

items.title: Perps will eat all of trading — $29T to $85.7T in 3 years, perps already 90% of crypto volume
items.idea: Global crypto perp trading volume grew from $29T (2023) to $58.5T (2024) to $85.7T (2025). Exchanges charge 2-5bps but on trillions this is gargantuan. Perps already consume ~90% of crypto volume. The bull case: perps generalize beyond crypto — any asset with an oracle price can be perped. US equities are open only 3.6% of the year. Perps are 24/7/365.
items.angle: The 90% volume stat is the key number. If the same dynamic plays out in traditional markets, perp exchanges become more valuable than traditional exchanges.
items.tweetA: Crypto perp volume: $29T to $58.5T to $85.7T. Three years. 3x. The US market was a rounding error in all that. Perps already took 90% of crypto. The argument that they do the same to TradFi isn't crazy — just uncomfortable.
items.tweetB: A perp doesn't care what it references. It just needs an oracle price. Stocks, oil, rate curves — all can be perped. The reason US equities are closed 96.4% of the year is institutional inertia, not necessity. That inertia just got a legislative challenge.

items.title: Perps flash crash risk: SPACEX-USDH wiped $1.5M in 30 minutes — the tail-wags-dog problem regulators fear
items.idea: This week, the Hyperliquid SPACEX-USDH perpetual flash-crashed, wiping ~$1.5M in notional in 30 minutes off one outsized position in thin liquidity. When 90% of volume is derivatives, you can overload a market that doesn't have the underlying spot liquidity to support it.
items.angle: The irony: this happens on the same day CFTC approves US perps. Watch for position limits and liquidity requirements in US perp regulation.
items.tweetA: Same week CFTC approves US perps: Hyperliquid's SPACEX-USDH perp flash-crashed $1.5M in 30 minutes on thin liquidity. This is why regulators hesitated. 90% derivatives volume + thin spot markets = the tail that can kill the dog. The regulation will catch up.
items.tweetB: Flash crash on a SpaceX perp. One outsized position. Thin liquidity. $1.5M gone in 30 minutes. This is the exact scenario that keeps regulators up at night — and now they've just approved the product class for US retail. Position limits are coming.

items.source: Bond Beat (via Yahoo Finance / Michael Burry)
items.title: Michael Burry: 'last months of the 1999-2000 bubble' — SOX up 3x from April 2025 low vs dot-com's 9x
items.idea: Michael Burry warns the market feels like 'the last months of the 1999-2000 bubble' with AI and semiconductor stocks at center. $SOX has tripled since April 2025 low. The dot-com Nasdaq Composite rose nearly 9x from its 1998 low before collapsing — current SOX gains are 3x smaller in scale. A trade can be right, early, and wildly overpriced all at the same time.
items.angle: The 3x vs 9x framing matters: either the bull market has 3x left OR the analog breaks at a lower magnitude. Burry's warning is about timing, not direction.
items.tweetA: SOX is up 3x from its April 2025 low. Dot-com Nasdaq ran 9x before it crashed. Michael Burry is calling it 'the last months of 1999.' One of these comparisons matters more than the other. The question is which direction you run with it.
items.tweetB: The Knicks are in the NBA Finals. Last time was 1999. The Nasdaq peaked shortly after. Correlation isn't causation, but Wall Street noticed. The actual question: is this a 3x move with 6x left, or a 3x move with 60% left?

charts.desc: SOX chart showing 3x run from April 2025 low — dot-com analog chart worth saving

items.source: Bond Beat (via BofA — The Thundering Word)
items.title: BofA: 5yr yield must fall below 3.25% to stabilize US debt; 30yr Treasury is worst 2020s asset at -54%
items.idea: BofA's 2026 global bond & equity primer: 5yr Treasury yield must fall below 3.25% to stabilize US debt interest costs — currently ~4.4%, requiring a 115bp rally. 30yr US Treasury down 54% peak-to-trough in 2020s — worst performing asset of the decade. US stock returns at all-time highs relative to US government bonds since 1900.
items.angle: The 3.25% number is the fiscal anchor. 'Controlled glide path' vs 'debt spiral' is a 115bp difference. The Fed cannot be genuinely dovish without fiscal justification.
items.tweetA: BofA math: 5yr Treasury needs to fall below 3.25% to stabilize US debt interest costs. It's currently at 4.4%. That's not a Fed cut scenario — that's a crisis scenario. The distance between 'controlled glide path' and 'debt spiral' is 115 basis points.
items.tweetB: The worst performing asset of the 2020s isn't crypto or meme stocks. It's the 30yr US Treasury. Down 54% peak-to-trough. Your pension fund held this. Your insurance company held this. And it's still not done repricing.

charts.desc: BofA 57-exhibit primer: stocks vs bonds relative performance since 1900 at all-time highs — structural chart worth saving

items.title: Deutsche Bank raises 10yr target to 4.70%; 82% of PCE basket hotter than 2018 — inflation is broad, not narrow
items.idea: Deutsche Bank raised 10yr UST target to 4.70% (from 4.45%). Key data: 82% of PCE basket running above 2018 inflation levels; only 18% softer. Net diffusion: +63 ppts. Supercore services ex-housing: 70% of categories hotter than 2018. Conclusion: breadth limits Fed's scope to resume cuts even if energy fades. Risks tilt toward a hike.
items.angle: The 82% breadth kills the 'inflation is just cars and airfares' narrative. Diffuse, embedded inflation across the basket is the strongest empirical argument against premature pivot.
items.tweetA: 82% of the PCE basket is running hotter than in 2018 — the last time inflation was sustainably at 2%. Not 82% of headline. 82% of the basket. Supercore services: 70% of categories still hot. Deutsche Bank just moved its 10yr target to 4.70% and said risks tilt toward a hike.
items.tweetB: The case for 'inflation is solved' requires you to explain how 82% of PCE categories are still running above 2018 levels. DB's answer: it can't be solved without broader demand destruction. The market is pricing soft landing. DB is pricing something structurally different.

charts.desc: DB inflation diffusion chart: 82% of PCE basket running above 2018 — key macro chart worth saving

items.title: Morgan Stanley: IG bond supply $300bn above average YTD — summer dearth is the tactical bull case for duration
items.idea: MS 5yr duration tactical long stopped out by IG supply: over $1T in 10yr Treasury equivalents YTD, $300bn above the 2023-25 average. Strategic bull view intact: IG supply dries up June-August. Historical seasonals (1990-2025): 10yr yields decline avg 18bp June to end of August. Strategy: treat higher yields as strategic duration opportunity; wait for May NFP first.
items.angle: The yield spike was supply-driven, not macro-driven. Supply turns off in 2 weeks. The trade is live if NFP disappoints.
items.tweetA: Investment grade bond supply is $300B above the 3yr average YTD. That's what pushed yields higher — not inflation, not the Fed. And it stops every June. Morgan Stanley: wait for NFP, then buy duration. Summer seasonals: -18bp on average in 10s.
items.tweetB: Morgan Stanley's tactical duration long failed — stopped out by IG supply. Strategic view unchanged: lower yields ahead. The summer window opens in 2 weeks when supply turns to dearth. The setup is real.

charts.desc: MS seasonal chart: 10yr yield -18bp avg June-August (1990-2025)

items.title: UBS: No Fed cut until March 2027; May NFP likely overstated; Chair Warsh argues AI is disinflationary
items.idea: UBS pushed first Fed cut to March 2027. Two sub-theses: (1) BEA income revisions suggest QCEW implies systematic NFP overstatement — underlying job growth ~50k/month. (2) Chair Warsh expected to argue AI's disinflationary power means Fed should discount backward-looking CPI data.
items.angle: NFP overstatement thesis is the pre-Friday-print trade. The Warsh AI-disinflation argument, if adopted, functionally creates a rate cut without cutting rates.
items.tweetA: UBS: First Fed cut is March 2027. Ten months away. And they're flagging that March and April's 'strong' payrolls may be overstated — BEA income revisions point to underlying job creation of ~50K/month. May NFP on Friday is the tell.
items.tweetB: Chair Warsh's argument: AI is disinflationary, so the Fed should discount backward-looking CPI data. It's the most intellectually interesting Fed thesis in years. If it gets traction, it's effectively a rate cut without cutting rates.

charts.desc: UBS balanced-approach rule chart vs projected funds rate

items.source: Bond Beat (via Hedgopia CoT — as of May 26, 2026)
items.title: Hedge funds: net short 788k 10yr contracts (covering); but adding 30yr shorts to 199.3k — a steepener bet
items.idea: CoT data May 26: Non-commercials net short 10yr at 788k contracts, down 60.1k WoW. 30yr bond shorts: 199.3k contracts, UP 20.6k WoW. 10yr rejected at October 2023 falling trendline (~4.69%), dropped to 4.45%. Next support: 50-day MA 4.39%, 200-day MA 4.20s.
items.angle: Divergence between 10s (covering) and 30s (adding) = bear steepener signal. HFs are rotating the short to the long end, not abandoning it.
items.tweetA: CoT: HFs net short 788K 10yr contracts — covered 60K this week after yield pullback from 4.69%. But in the 30yr, shorts are INCREASING — up 20.6K to 199.3K. This isn't a bond bull capitulation. It's a steepener bet.
items.tweetB: 10yr rejected at 4.69%, dropped to 4.45%. HFs covered. But they're adding 30yr shorts. Bear steepener is the positioning consensus. The curve agrees — fiscal math hits the long end harder than the front end.

items.title: Barclays: US-Iran deal imminent; May NFP forecast 75k vs 93k consensus — consumers feeling real purchasing power strain
items.idea: Barclays calls US-Iran deal 'imminent.' Trump met Iranian team Friday without signing. US consumer: purchasing power pressures intensified, spending softening. Barclays May NFP: 75k vs consensus 93k; unemployment 4.3%. Tension: single-month prints 115-183k in March-April vs 3mma of only 48k/month.
items.angle: One of the most aggressive below-consensus NFP calls. 75k vs 93k = bond yields rally, dollar weakens on the miss.
items.tweetA: Barclays is calling May NFP at 75k vs consensus 93k. March was 183k. April was 115k. The 3-month average is 48k. One of these numbers represents reality. Friday's print will tell you which one — and bonds will move hard on the answer.
items.tweetB: US-Iran deal is imminent per Barclays. Trump met Friday, left without signing. If the deal lands this weekend: oil collapses, gold spikes, bond yields rally. Every hour without a signature keeps geopolitical vol premium in the market.

items.title: Deutsche Bank: S&P at bottom of 3.5yr trend channel; Iran and earnings catalysts 'done' — rotation setup
items.idea: DB positioning: S&P caught up to bottom of 3.5-year trend channel 3 weeks ago, edging higher along it. Both catalysts (Iran de-escalation, Q1 earnings) 'look to be done.' Large-cap Tech positioning stretched. Buybacks and inflows robust. Call: 'slower choppier grind up or pause with rotation into lagging sectors and regions.'
items.angle: Both catalysts exhausted + large-cap Tech stretched = next 10% needs new leadership. Rotation into lagging regions (Europe? EM?) and sectors (energy, financials) is the DB trade.
items.tweetA: Deutsche Bank: the two catalysts that drove the S&P recovery — Iran de-escalation and Q1 earnings — are both now 'done.' Large-cap Tech is stretched. The market grinds along the bottom of its 3.5yr trend channel. DB's call: rotation, not reversal.
items.tweetB: S&P at the bottom of its trend channel, running on fumes from exhausted catalysts. DB sees favorable supply-demand (buybacks + inflows) but stretched Mag7. If you've been hiding in large-cap Tech: what's been lagging for 18 months?

charts.desc: DB S&P 500 trend channel chart — worth opening

items.title: Gold: 4-month correction within secular bull intact; Iran deal = sharp catalyst; support $4,300-$4,600
items.idea: Gold in multi-wave correction since late January but 200-day MA still upward sloping — uptrend intact. Williams %R deeply oversold — same setup preceded March and April rebounds. Key support zone: $4,300-$4,600. Iran-related developments are primary near-term driver. Analyst: gold is only 2 years into a decade-long secular bull market.
items.angle: Iran deal timing is everything. Technical setup constructive regardless. Miners (GDX, GDXJ) have more leverage than physical on a sharp move.
items.tweetA: Gold is deeply oversold on Williams %R — same setup as March and April, both of which preceded significant rebounds. 200-day MA still pointing up. Support: $4,300-$4,600. Catalyst sitting on Trump's desk: Iran peace deal. This weekend matters.
items.tweetB: If gold is 2 years into a decade-long secular bull driven by fiscal math and de-dollarization, then every Iran-driven dip to oversold levels is the entry. The noise is geopolitical. The thesis is structural.

charts.desc: Gold, silver, and miners technical charts with Williams %R oversold levels — setup charts worth saving

items.title: Claude Opus 4.8: 84% on Mind2Web, 4x fewer code review failures, mid-task instructions without breaking cache
items.idea: Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.8 tuned for agentic judgment. 84% on Online-Mind2Web. ~4x less likely to overlook code flaws vs Opus 4.7. New: effort controls; dynamic workflows; Systems API allowing mid-task instruction changes without breaking prompt cache. Fast mode: 3x cheaper, 2.5x faster. Pricing unchanged ($5/$25 per million tokens). Available now via claude-opus-4-8.
items.angle: The Systems API cache-preservation detail is undersold. In long-horizon agentic tasks, instructions change mid-run — previously this forced a full context restart. This is a production reliability improvement.
items.tweetA: Claude Opus 4.8: 84% on Mind2Web. 4x fewer code review failures. But the real advance is the Systems API — you can change instructions mid-task without breaking the prompt cache. For long-running agents, this is the difference between 'mostly works' and 'works reliably.'
items.tweetB: Anthropic is optimizing Opus for agents, not chat. Effort controls, dynamic workflows, better self-reporting of progress, mid-task instruction changes. This isn't a consumer product upgrade. It's production infrastructure for autonomous AI.

items.title: AutoScientists (Harvard): self-organizing multi-agent science teams — 74.4% biomedical ML, 1.9x faster convergence, learns from failure
items.idea: Harvard's Zitnik Lab: AutoScientists — decentralized multi-agent system where agents self-organize around promising directions instead of fixed plans. Vets proposals before allocating compute. Results: 74.4% mean leaderboard percentile on biomedical ML; 1.9x faster convergence on LM training; protein fitness gains. Key innovation: documents failures to steer future exploration — builds institutional memory.
items.angle: First published system that treats failure data as first-class input. Self-organization + failure memory = smarter about where NOT to look. Direct map to drug discovery and materials science.
items.tweetA: Harvard's AutoScientists: multi-agent system that self-organizes and documents failures. 74.4% biomedical ML percentile. 1.9x faster convergence. The failure documentation is the real innovation — it builds memory from what doesn't work.
items.tweetB: The bottleneck in scientific discovery isn't compute — it's knowing which experiments have already failed. AutoScientists solves that by making failure a first-class signal. That's how you get 1.9x faster convergence.

items.title: Agent harness scaling laws: non-monotone sensitivity — more scaffolding can degrade performance
items.idea: New research: scaling laws for agent harnesses show non-monotone sensitivity — adding more evaluation scaffolding does not linearly improve and some configurations degrade performance. SIA (co-updating harness and model weights simultaneously) outperforms updating either alone. Also: Polar trains agents on real harnesses; CUA-Gym scales computer-use RL data.
items.angle: Non-monotone sensitivity is counterintuitive and practically important for anyone building agent infrastructure with layered safety/eval checks. The harness is a trainable component, not fixed infrastructure.
items.tweetA: New result: agent harness scaling laws are non-monotone. More scaffolding doesn't linearly improve agent performance — some configurations actively degrade it. If you're building agent infrastructure with layered evals, this changes your architecture assumptions.
items.tweetB: SIA: co-update the model weights AND the harness simultaneously — outperforms tuning either alone. The harness isn't fixed infrastructure. It's a trainable component. This blurs the line between agent and system.

items.title: Prediction markets as probability sensors: real-time, skin-in-the-game — but 3 unsolved problems remain
items.idea: a16z: prediction markets are fundamental information aggregation mechanisms, isolating single event probabilities unlike commodities. Real-time, incentivized, updatable. New use case: markets for AI model performance by task — too micro for traditional markets. Three unsolved problems: (1) oracle/resolution at scale, (2) informed participant selection (Brexit/Trump 2016 may have been underpriced due to participant bias), (3) manipulation risk (partially self-correcting).
items.angle: The AI model performance prediction market could become the real-time leaderboard that matters more than official benchmarks — monetizing information asymmetry between hands-on users and press release readers.
items.tweetA: a16z buries the killer app: prediction markets for which AI model performs best at which task. Too micro for commodity markets. Real-time leaderboard where people with actual hands-on knowledge get paid to be right. That signal beats any published benchmark.
items.tweetB: Prediction markets underestimated Brexit and Trump 2016 because the people trading weren't plugged into populism. The failure mode is systematic: you only aggregate the beliefs of the people who show up. Solve participant selection and you solve geopolitical forecasting.

items.title: AI agents bypassing SaaS entirely — clean API infrastructure wins; B2B incumbents running out of time
items.idea: Infrastructure companies with clean APIs are gaining share because agents interact directly with systems instead of navigating human-focused interfaces. SaaS UIs are becoming liabilities rather than moats. Structural pressure on application-layer software whose functionality can be reconstructed by agents using cheaper infrastructure. Lemkin: pick one painful workflow, build a deeply integrated agent around proprietary data, monetize through installed base.
items.angle: UI complexity is now a negative moat. The investor due diligence question changes: 'can an agent do this job with three API calls?'
items.tweetA: AI agents don't use UIs. They call APIs. The moat that took SaaS companies 10 years to build — intuitive interfaces, user flows, onboarding — is now a liability. Infrastructure companies with clean APIs are gaining share. The application layer is being commoditized from below.
items.tweetB: The SaaS disruption thesis used to be theoretical. Now it's measurable: companies with clean APIs win deals agents route around human-facing apps. The investor question: 'can an agent do this job with three API endpoints instead?'

items.title: Amazon ($747B revenue) vs SpaceX ($15B): both ~$2T valuation — market pricing infrastructure dominance over cash flow
items.idea: Amazon generates $747B in annual revenue; SpaceX $15B annually. Both valued at ~$2 trillion. The valuation parity reflects markets pricing future infrastructure dominance. SpaceX at $2T on $15B revenue = 133x sales; Amazon = 2.7x. The gap is 50x in revenue, zero in market cap.
items.angle: Either SpaceX's TAM is 50x larger than Amazon's, or one of these is priced wrong. The market is pricing monopoly returns on future payload delivery and direct-to-device connectivity. That's either visionary or delusional with no middle ground.
items.tweetA: Amazon: $747B revenue, $2T market cap. SpaceX: $15B revenue, $2T market cap. Same valuation. That's a pure TAM bet — the market says space infrastructure has the same ceiling as global logistics + cloud. Maybe. But the burden of proof is on $15B.
items.tweetB: SpaceX at $2T on $15B revenue is a 133x sales multiple. NVDA trades at ~30x. Amazon at 2.7x. The market is pricing the end state of a monopoly on payload delivery to orbit and direct-to-device connectivity. Either visionary or delusional. There is no middle ground.

items.title: Uber hits AI spend ceiling — token consumption no longer maps to product output or customer value
items.idea: Uber executives stated rising AI token consumption no longer maps cleanly to better product output or customer value. Internal conversation shifted from 'how much AI are we using' to 'what measurable work is getting done.' One of the most data-sophisticated tech companies reporting diminishing marginal returns on AI token spend at scale.
items.angle: If Uber can't find incremental product value from additional token spend, the 'AI makes everything better with more spend' phase may be ending. This should hit AI infrastructure spend assumptions before it hits earnings.
items.tweetA: Uber says: rising token spend is no longer mapping to product improvement. This isn't a small company fumbling AI. It's one of the most sophisticated data operations in tech saying returns are diminishing at the margin. The hyperscaler CapEx models assume this never happens.
items.tweetB: Every hyperscaler CapEx announcement assumes AI spend = productivity gain. Uber is the first major enterprise to publicly say: we've hit a ceiling. When that gap closes, the spending slows.

items.source: The Founders Corner (via Jason Saltzman — analysis of 342 US roles)
items.title: Higher earners have higher AI exposure — median pay rises from $39K (low exposure) to $84K (high exposure)
items.idea: Analysis of 342 US roles: AI exposure rises with income. Median pay moves from $39K (low exposure) to $84K (highest exposure). Work patterns once protected by expertise are now more machine-readable. This inverts the historical automation pattern where lower-skilled, lower-paid work is hit first.
items.angle: Direct implications for professional services — law, finance, medicine, consulting. The people most likely to be disrupted are also the most likely AI buyers. The market prices them as winners, not losers.
items.tweetA: Which jobs are most exposed to AI? The ones paying $84K, not $39K. Analysis of 342 US roles: AI exposure rises with income. The complexity that made expertise a moat is exactly what makes it most machine-readable. The knowledge worker assumption was wrong.
items.tweetB: We spent a decade worrying AI would replace truck drivers and factory workers. The data says it's replacing the $84K median roles first — analysts, researchers, lawyers, writers. The safety was never in complexity. It was in physical presence.

charts.desc: Income vs AI exposure distribution chart — worth opening

items.title: Replit: 3 YC rejections → $9B valuation in six months from $3B; 750K to 50M users
items.idea: Replit rejected by YC three times, now at $9B (up from $3B in 6 months). 750K to 50M users. Founder Amjad Masad started after debugging environment failures at Yahoo. Also: Atomus raising $500M VC fund; Transition Ventures closed $150M for climate/energy in Europe; Longwall Ventures Fund IV up to $50M from British Business Bank (deeptech).
items.angle: 750K to 50M users is ~67x user expansion — valuation is lagging the user metric. At 50M developers, this approaches GitHub-scale density. Monetization per developer is the key question.
items.tweetA: Replit: rejected by YC 3 times. Now at $9B, up 3x in six months. 50M users, up from 750K. The total market for code execution environments became infinite when AI could write code. Replit happened to be positioned.
items.tweetB: From 750K to 50M users. $3B to $9B valuation. Six months. The Replit number that matters isn't the valuation — it's user density. At 50M developers, monetization per user determines whether this is a $9B or $90B company.

items.title: VanEck JEDI space ETF up 128% in 6 months — Planet Labs, Rocket Lab, AST SpaceMobile, Intuitive Machines in the basket
items.idea: VanEck Space Innovators UCITS ETF (JEDI) up 128% in 6 months. Holdings: Planet Labs (Earth observation fleet), Rocket Lab, Viasat, EchoStar, Globalstar (Apple Emergency SOS), AST SpaceMobile (direct-to-smartphone satellite cellular, no special hardware), Firefly Aerospace, Intuitive Machines (Moon landing 2024, NASA lunar logistics), Sky Perfect JSAT, Iridium (only true global coverage). Author: 'buying here is a different bet than buying where I did.'
items.angle: Convergence of SpaceX IPO expectations, satellite internet demand, Apple dependency on Globalstar, and direct-to-phone connectivity are all live simultaneously inside JEDI. The sector graduated from niche to infrastructure.
items.tweetA: VanEck space ETF (JEDI) is up 128% in six months. Inside it: Rocket Lab, AST SpaceMobile building satellite cellular connecting to regular phones with no special hardware, Intuitive Machines that landed on the moon. This is an industry now, not a theme.
items.tweetB: SpaceX isn't in JEDI — it's private. But the ecosystem it's enabling IS there. AST SpaceMobile for direct-to-phone. Intuitive Machines for lunar logistics. Iridium for military/maritime. 128% is not the ceiling. It's what happened before the IPO cycle opens.

items.title: Bitcoin buy order at $58K after breakdown from $80K; crypto airdrop farming now 100x more competitive due to AI bots
items.idea: Bitcoin buy order placed at $58K after breakdown from $80K. Still holding $HYPE, $ZEC, $VVV. Key insight: crypto airdrop farming is now 100x more competitive vs a few years ago — competing with AI agents and bots. Personal lesson: was a TOP 1% Solstice farmer but missed the claim window due to poor team communication. Separately: considering trimming S&P 500 and World ETF positions on bubble concerns.
items.angle: The $58K BTC level is well-known support. The airdrop farming insight is more important: AI bots have commoditized airdrop farming, reducing human alpha in that strategy to near-zero.
items.tweetA: Retail crypto trader placing a $BTC buy at $58K after breakdown from $80K. Meanwhile: airdrop farming is now 100x more competitive because you're competing with AI agents. Two data points on the same theme — easy crypto money required being early and human.
items.tweetB: Best airdrop of the past months launched, distributed, and closed its claim window before a top-1% farmer noticed. Crypto alpha is increasingly gatekept by information flow management, not farming volume. The edge moved up the stack.

items.title: AI-generated newsletters flooding Substack under anonymous pseudonyms charging for content — authenticity is now scarce
items.idea: Herb Greenberg identifies a growing phenomenon: sophisticated-seeming investing newsletters written by anonymous pseudonyms, remarkably prolific, charging for content. Process: AI prompts + tweak AI-isms = 'differentiated content.' Paul Graham independently said the same about AI founder emails. Greenberg's counter: writing is part of discovery — AI can assist research (he uses Gemini, Perplexity, Claude, Tenzing MEMO, Quartr) but replacing the writing loses the thinking. 'AI brain fry' from verification is real.
items.angle: Greenberg uses AI as research infrastructure = competitive advantage. Fake writers use AI as writing infrastructure = commoditized output. Differentiator is now what you can verify, not what you know.
items.tweetA: Herb Greenberg identifies the fake newsletter playbook: anonymous pseudonym + AI prompts + tweak AI-isms = 'sophisticated' investing content. Paul Graham said the same about AI founder emails. Authenticity is now the scarce signal. The market for verifiable human voice is tightening.
items.tweetB: Writing is part of thinking. If you outsource the writing, you lose the discovery. Greenberg spends hours verifying. The AI-written newsletters skip that. The difference between research and AI-assisted research is entirely in the verification layer.

items.title: Dell beats by $10B; Anthropic at $65B — the unknowable AI market-clearing level keeps all guesses missing low
items.idea: Dell beat revenue estimates by over $10 billion. Anthropic closed at $65B valuation. VC friend: 'for VCs, it is a parlor game — they just really bet on public market flips now.' Counterpoint: 'the unknown of the level at which the market for AI goods and services will finally clear is what keeps valuations moving up, because we can only guess at the level, and to date the guesses have all missed low.'
items.angle: The 'guesses have all missed low' observation is the most honest articulation of the AI bull case — not irrational exuberance, but rational uncertainty about a demand ceiling that keeps revising upward. The bear case requires knowing something 10 consecutive cohorts missed.
items.tweetA: Dell beat revenue estimates by $10 billion. Not 10%. $10 billion. And Anthropic just closed at $65B. The AI market-clearing level keeps being revised up, and to date every single guess has missed low.
items.tweetB: The honest AI bull case: nobody knows where demand stops. Until you can model the ceiling, you cannot know what 'overpriced' means. Anthropic at $65B might be cheap if demand 10x's again. The bear case requires knowing something that 10 consecutive cohorts of forecasters got wrong.

items.title: Less than 10% of quant researchers at Millennium, Citadel, Point72 ever reach PM — it is structural, not alpha quality
items.idea: Fewer than 10% of researchers at large multi-manager platforms ever reach PM seat internally. The majority move laterally, plateau as senior researchers, or leave within a few years. Cause is structural: researcher role and PM role are two distinct functions inside the same firm. The distance between holding a research seat and running a book is wider than most understand when accepting their first research offer.
items.angle: If alpha quality does not determine PM selection at the world's best quant shops, what does? This matters for allocators — you're betting on PM selection mechanics, not just alpha process. It also implies massive talent inefficiency and explains the emerging quant fund landscape.
items.tweetA: Fewer than 10% of quant researchers at Millennium, Citadel, and Point72 ever make it to PM. The reason has very little to do with their alpha quality. The gap between 'generating good signals' and 'running a book' is structural, not meritocratic.
items.tweetB: Pod shop PM selection is the black box inside the black box. Top quant platforms promote fewer than 10% of researchers internally. The talent pipeline leaks to fund launches, competitors, and burnout. This is a structural inefficiency — and it explains a lot about the quant fund landscape.
